topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The rhythmic thud of the hammer against the post sounded like a drumbeat in the quiet afternoon. I stood by the window, clutching a lukewarm mug of tea so tightly my knuckles were white. Every time the neighbor, Mr. Henderson, paused to wipe his brow and glance over at my house, a cold knot tightened in my stomach. He was a large man, and his enthusiasm for this new boundary line seemed almost aggressive. I kept smoothing the wrinkles from my cardigan, a futile attempt to make myself smaller, less visible. *Just let him finish,* I pleaded silently to the plaster wall. The sound of the saw starting up made me flinch, my breath catching in my throat.

[story 2]

"Just a little closer to the property line, that's all," Mr. Henderson chirped, his voice unnervingly cheerful as he gestured towards the overgrown hedge. I managed a tight, practiced smile, my gaze fixed on the distant lawn. My hands began to fidget, picking at loose threads on my sleeve. The sheer scale of the project—the digging, the noise, the sudden alteration of my established view—felt overwhelming. I started pacing the small living room, my steps quick and uneven, unable to settle my feet. I kept glancing at the gate, half-expecting him to appear at any moment, demanding my attention.

[story 3]

I watched the scaffolding go up, a towering, metallic intrusion against the familiar backdrop of our street. A tremor ran through my hands as I watched the first section of the fence being erected. My jaw felt tight, locked in a rigid line. I retreated to the kitchen, pretending to check the oven, but my eyes kept darting toward the construction site. Each clang of metal on metal sent a jolt through me. I took a deep, shaky breath, trying to regulate the rapid flutter in my chest. When Mr. Henderson finally called out, "Everything is going smoothly!" my response came out as a strained whisper, "Yes. Very smooth."
